title: Slab-built cylindrical tea caddy
subtitle: A lidded cylinder for loose-leaf tea, built from a rolled slab
excerpt: A cylindrical tea caddy built from a single rolled slab wrapped around a cardboard former and fitted with a push-in lid. Air-dry clay throughout, no kiln required. The finished caddy stores loose-leaf tea or dried herbs away from light. Finished height approximately 120 mm, diameter 80 mm.
sourceNotes: Slab-cylinder technique derived from first principles. Cardboard-former method is a standard studio teaching approach. Encyclopedia Britannica 11th edition (1911), pottery entry, for historical context on lidded storage vessels.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat sheet of clay rolled to an even thickness and cut to shape.
The drying stage where clay holds its shape but is still damp enough to trim and join.
Scratching the joining surfaces with a needle tool and brushing them with clay slurry before pressing together.
Fully air-dried and no longer damp. For air-dry clay, this is the structural finish state.

content.text: A cylindrical tea caddy is a practical slab-building exercise. One
slab

is wrapped around a cardboard former to form the body, a disc closes the base, and a second disc sits inside the mouth as a push-in lid. Air-dry clay hardens at room temperature. The finished caddy is suitable for loose-leaf tea, dried herbs, or small dry goods kept away from direct light. It is not waterproof, so do not use it for anything wet.

items.name: Fettling knife
items.substitution: Craft knife with a long blade

items.name: Cardboard tube, 80 mm diameter
items.substitution: Rolled cardboard wrapped in cling film

content.text: Preparation
Wrap the cardboard tube in a single layer of cling film. This allows the clay to release from the former once it stiffens to
leather-hard
. Keep a small jar of water nearby for making slip: a teaspoon of clay dissolved in water to a thick-cream consistency.
Rolling and cutting the panels

Place the slab guides on the wooden board 200 mm apart. Roll the clay between them to 6 mm thickness, rolling from the centre outward and rotating the slab a quarter-turn between each pass. The surface should be smooth with no ridges.

Cut three pieces from the rolled slab. First, one rectangle 260 mm long by 120 mm wide for the body wall. Second, one disc 80 mm in diameter for the base. Third, one disc 74 mm in diameter for the lid. The lid disc is 6 mm smaller in diameter than the opening so it sits inside the mouth rather than sitting on top of it.
Forming the cylinder

Set the rectangular slab on the board and place the wrapped cardboard tube at one short end. Lift the board gently to help the clay curl around the tube. Press the clay against the tube with both hands, working from one end to the other. The two long edges of the rectangle will overlap by about 10 mm at the seam.
At the seam, apply
score and slip
: scratch both overlapping surfaces with the needle tool, brush on a thin layer of slip, then press firmly. Smooth the exterior seam with the wooden rib until no visible line remains.
Attaching the base

Stand the wrapped cylinder upright with the tube inside. Score and slip the bottom edge of the cylinder wall and the rim of the 80 mm base disc. Press the base disc into position. Work the joint from the inside with a fingertip, pressing clay from the wall down onto the base in a smooth curve. Repeat on the outside, closing any gap.
Stiffening and releasing the former

Leave the cylinder upright with the tube inside until the clay reaches leather-hard, around 3-4 hours at room temperature. The clay will pull slightly away from the cling film as it shrinks. Slide the tube out by gripping the tube ends and pulling straight down. The cling film releases cleanly.
Smoothing and drying

Once the tube is out, smooth the interior walls with a damp fingertip or the sponge. Wipe the exterior lightly with the wooden rib to close any surface cracks. Cover loosely with a plastic bag and leave to dry for 2-3 days, turning the caddy once during drying to prevent uneven shrinkage on one side.
Fitting the lid
The lid disc dries flat on the board. Once
bone-dry

, test the fit. The 74 mm disc should sit inside the 80 mm mouth with a small gap all around. If it sits too loose, press a thin coil of clay around the underside rim of the lid, let it stiffen, and test again. If it sits too tight, sand the lid edge gently with fine sandpaper.
Finishing

Sand the exterior lightly with fine sandpaper to remove rough patches. Apply two coats of PVA sealer or clear acrylic varnish, allowing each coat to dry fully. The sealer strengthens the surface and provides a degree of moisture resistance, though the caddy remains for dry storage only.
Care

Air-dry clay is not waterproof. Keep the caddy away from direct moisture. Wipe the exterior with a barely damp cloth if needed. Do not immerse in water. The sealed surface is stable for everyday dry-storage use.
Variations
Texture the slab before wrapping: press a lace strip or pottery stamp into the flat clay before forming the cylinder.
Add a lid knob by pressing a small ball of clay onto the lid centre and fixing it with score and slip.
Make a trio of smaller caddies using tubes of 50 mm, 60 mm, and 80 mm diameter for a graduated set.

rows.problem: Cylinder cracks at the seam during drying
rows.cause: The seam was not compressed firmly enough, or clay dried too fast
rows.fix: Mend with fresh slip and press firmly. Cover with plastic for a slower, more even dry.

rows.cause: Insufficient score and slip, or the base was attached when the wall was already too stiff
rows.fix: Re-wet the joint edge, score and re-slip, press firmly, and work clay from the inside with a fingertip.

rows.cause: Clay was too soft when the former was removed
rows.fix: Leave the cardboard tube inside longer. The clay should feel firm and hold its shape before removal.

rows.problem: Lid does not fit the opening
rows.cause: Uneven drying shrank the caddy more on one side
rows.fix: Sand the lid edge gently on fine sandpaper. For a snug fit, the lid should require a gentle push to seat.
